coredata.dc:title: Fluorene-naphthalene Schiff base as a smart pigment in invisible ink with multiple security features for advanced anticounterfeiting and forensic applications

coredata.dc:description: Smart functional materials with captivating optical properties are of immense importance due to their versatile applicability in anticounterfeiting and forensic science. A fluorene-naphthalene Schiff base (FNH) that displays aggregation induced emission, mechanofluorochromism and excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence inherent to the pristine and ground samples is synthesized. Water/solvent-based invisible security inks for flexo/screen printing were formulated using FNH as a smart pigment to check the originality of documents/branded products etc. The prints with good photostability, adherence to substrate and rub resistance are invisible in daylight showcasing multiple non-destructive and destructive techniques to authenticate the document. The inked area on UV dull paper substrate exhibits a weak emission, which is observed by the forger under UVA light. However, the user can validate the authenticity of the document by rubbing the print with hard objects, especially using a metal coin or glass rod to perceive a human eye detectable intensification in the orange fluorescence under the same illumination source. The intensity of the orange fluorescence reverts to the original, which enables the reuse of the security document after originality check. Yet another nondestructive authentication method is to observe a cyan fluorescence from the print and orangish yellow fluorescence from the rubbed printed region when shined with a 270–400 nm light source, whereas a cyanish green fluorescence both from the unrubbed and rubbed regions of the print when illuminated with a visible light source ranging from 420 to 480 nm. An additional verification through a destructive technique is to perceive red and yellow fluorescence of the ink film upon contact with THF and NaOH/KOH, respectively and a penetrating red fluorescence from the rear side of the THF-exposed printed area of the paper. The multi-level security features that cannot be easily replicated by the forger but allows a simple and easy validation process by the user are unique to FNH, used as a single pigment in the inks. Further, the applicability of the ground FNH in forensic science is established to distinctly observe Level I to II details of latent fingerprints.

head.ce:title: Fluorene-naphthalene Schiff base as a smart pigment in invisible ink with multiple security features for advanced anticounterfeiting and forensic applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Smart functional materials with captivating optical properties are of immense importance due to their versatile applicability in anticounterfeiting and forensic science. A fluorene-naphthalene Schiff base (FNH) that displays aggregation induced emission, mechanofluorochromism and excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence inherent to the pristine and ground samples is synthesized. Water/solvent-based invisible security inks for flexo/screen printing were formulated using FNH as a smart pigment to check the originality of documents/branded products etc. The prints with good photostability, adherence to substrate and rub resistance are invisible in daylight showcasing multiple non-destructive and destructive techniques to authenticate the document. The inked area on UV dull paper substrate exhibits a weak emission, which is observed by the forger under UVA light. However, the user can validate the authenticity of the document by rubbing the print with hard objects, especially using a metal coin or glass rod to perceive a human eye detectable intensification in the orange fluorescence under the same illumination source. The intensity of the orange fluorescence reverts to the original, which enables the reuse of the security document after originality check. Yet another nondestructive authentication method is to observe a cyan fluorescence from the print and orangish yellow fluorescence from the rubbed printed region when shined with a 270–400 nm light source, whereas a cyanish green fluorescence both from the unrubbed and rubbed regions of the print when illuminated with a visible light source ranging from 420 to 480 nm. An additional verification through a destructive technique is to perceive red and yellow fluorescence of the ink film upon contact with THF and NaOH/KOH, respectively and a penetrating red fluorescence from the rear side of the THF-exposed printed area of the paper. The multi-level security features that cannot be easily replicated by the forger but allows a simple and easy validation process by the user are unique to FNH, used as a single pigment in the inks. Further, the applicability of the ground FNH in forensic science is established to distinctly observe Level I to II details of latent fingerprints.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Invisible security ink for anticounterfeiting, Latent fingerprint detection

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The supplementary data and Video are provided. The data generated and/or analyzed during the study are available from the first author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the contemporary world, illegal counterfeiting of currency notes and security documents has created profound economic damage to both government and private sectors. Moreover, the protection of branded products, pharmaceuticals, and medicines that has immense societal impact call for investment of significant research efforts to develop novel materials and smart techniques to combat forgery [1,2]. The creation of fake documents/banknotes and forged items demands development of unique inks with difficult-to-copy security features for the forger to replicate. Various approaches implemented to authenticate original documents/goods are associated with different mechanisms to detect forgery. Moreover, the inclusion of exclusive security features that could be used to easily confirm the authenticity of the document/product, but are known only to the user is always sought after to maintain the integrity of these valuable items. In this context, fluorescent molecular systems that respond to various stimuli including solvents, pressure/force, heat, humidity, UV/NIR illumination etc. to display different emission colors can be exploited as smart materials for numerous optical counterfeiting applications [3–5]. These stimuli-induced materials have garnered substantial research interest and their fluorescence (FL) behavior can be altered through various strategies such as breakage and formation of bonds, disruption of intermolecular interactions, variations in molecular packing arrangements, changes in ordered molecular assemblies, and rearrangements in molecular conformations [6]. Among the various stimuli-responsive fluorophores, mechanofluorochromic materials with bright solid-state emission, generally attributed to aggregation-induced emission (AIE) or enhanced emission (AIEE) have aroused immense attention [7–10]. However, it is highly challenging to develop them by overcoming aggregation-induced FL quenching (ACQ). Novel molecular designs can promote the persistent progress of advanced multifunctional force-induced fluorophores that can showcase remarkable FL performances towards security ink and anticounterfeiting.

In the pursuit of new functional molecular systems, fluorene is a white, crystalline, rigid, and planar ortho-fused tricyclic aromatic hydrocarbon with a characteristic violet FL that has not been explored much. Though the ACQ effect mostly hampers the real-life applications of fluorene derivatives [11], few mechanofluorochromic and AIE active fluorene-based compounds/complexes/polymers are reported (Supplementary Table S1) as chemical sensors, pressure detectors and light-emitting materials in organic light emitting diodes [3,11–17]. However, fluorene-incorporated molecular frameworks have never been exploited as smart mechanochromic fluorescent pigments in security inks towards anticounterfeiting. Though a fluorene-based fluorescent pigment FBH for anticounterfeit application have been reported in our earlier study [18], the molecule did not exhibit any mechanochromic features. Several imines are generally known to exhibit AIE and mechanofluorochromic properties [19,20]. Hence we replaced the salicyloyl hydrazide unit in FBH with a bulkier 3-hydroxy-2-naphthohydrazide group to obtain a new fluorene and naphthalene-derived Schiff base (FNH) with the intention of introducing mechanochromic and AIE features. FNH is synthesized for use as an intelligent functional colorant to formulate both solvent and water-based inks for screen and flexography printing. A single pigment that owns the following novel features: i) faintly emissive in its pristine form, but displays intense orange FL under UVA source upon rubbing, ii) heat induced reversal of rub-induced FL emission, iii) AIE, iv) excitation wavelength dependent FL trails that are different for the pristine and ground samples and v) solvent/pH-sensitive FL have not been reported yet. FNH that displays these multiple stimuli-responsive FL features limits the need for two or more pigments that are generally used in an ink formulation to achieve the desired high-level security features. The intrinsic and unique covert security features exhibited by the FNH ink film combined with its good adhesion, photostability and rub resistance on UV dull paper substrates are demonstrated in this comprehensive study. Further, to alleviate the toxicity and sensitivity concerns associated with luminescent materials that are currently used for latent fingerprint identification [21], the ability of biocompatible FNH to be utilized as a FL-based platform in the visualization and preservation of latent fingerprint (LFPs) is also explored. Therefore, this study is novel in reporting a new fluorene-based functional material as a biocompatible luminophore in fingerprint analysis and as a single pigment that can impart robust multi-level covert features in its solvent/water-based ink films on paper substrates for advanced anticounterfeiting. These security features can be suitably used, wherein the originality of the printed documents/products can be validated through non-destructible (allowing the reuse of documents/goods) as well as destructible techniques. Moreover, the user can easily check the multiple covert security features that are masked to the forger and difficult to identify or replicate.

FNH was designed by grafting a 3-hydroxy 2-naphthohydrazide to the fluorene core through a one-step Schiff base reaction. The synthesis of FNH involved a simple condensation reaction as presented in Scheme 1 .
N'-((9H-fluoren-2-yl)methylene)-3-hydroxy-2-naphthohydrazide (FNH).

Equimolar mixture of fluorene-2-carboxaldehyde (1 mmol) and 3-hydroxy-2-naphthohydrazide (1 mmol) dissolved in methanol was refluxed at 65 ℃ for 6 h. The off-white precipitate formed was filtered, washed with methanol, dried and recrystallized to obtain FNH (Scheme 1).

The melting point of FNH was determined using an open capillary method and is uncorrected. The molecular structure of FNH was confirmed by recording ATR-FTIR (Shimadzu – IR Spirit analyzer), NMR (DMSO‑d6, 400 MHz Bruker spectrometer) and mass (Xevo QToF, Waters) spectra. The chemical characterization data of FNH, synthesized in good yield is in agreement with its designed structure.

The single crystals of FNH were grown in tetrahydrofuran (THF) by slow evaporation method at room temperature for X-ray data collection. The single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXRD) data were acquired at 300 K using a Bruker AXS area-detector diffractometer with graphite monochromated MoKα radiation (λ = 0.71073 Å). Direct method algorithm of SHELXS97 [22] was used to solve the crystal structure and further refined using full-matrix least squares on F2 with SHELXL-2019 [23]. All carbon (C) bound hydrogen (H) atoms were positioned at calculated locations and were refined on their parent atoms with Uisoset to 1.2Ueq. Oxygen (O) and Nitrogen (N) bound H atoms were placed in a difference Fourier map and refined isotropically with Uisoset to 1.2Ueq of the carrier N atom and with Uisoset to 1.5Ueq of O atom. CrystalExplorer (Version 17.5) software was used for Hirshfeld surface (HS) analysis, including 3D HS maps, 2D fingerprint plots [24], and electrostatic energy frameworks [25] to probe the intermolecular interactions of FNH.

The electronic spectra were acquired using 1800 Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer and JASCO spectrofluorometer FP 8300. The optical microscopic images of FNH and ground FNH (FNH-g) were captured using a Euromex iScope series 110–240 V/50–60 Hz with 100x magnification. Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument was used to check the particle size of FNH aggregates. The thermal studies were performed using Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC) 3 STARe System, Mettler Toledo, and Thermogravimetric Analyser (TGA) 4000, PerkinElmer, at a heating rate of 20 °C per minute. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data was acquired using Rigaku Miniflex 600 (5th gen) from 5 to 80° at 4° per minute. The morphological features were imaged using Carl Zeiss EVO 18 analytical scanning electron microscope (SEM). B3LYP functions of Schrödinger's Materials Science Suite with a 6-31G++ basis set in vacuum was used to perform the computational simulations.

The cytotoxicity of FNH was examined in HeLa cells by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. HeLa cells cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM, CELL clone) containing 10% Foetal Bovine Serum (Invitrogen), Penicillin (100 units/mL) and Streptomycin (100 μg/mL) were incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 humidified incubator (Sanyo). About 1 × 104 cells per well (96 well plate) were seeded, incubated for 24 h and treated with FNH. The cells were washed after 24 h incubation, MTT (20 μL; 5 mg/mL in DMEM) was added and incubated for another 3 h in the dark. Finally, DMSO (100 μL) was added to the cells after removing the medium, incubated for 5 min in the dark and absorbance (560 nm) was measured to calculate the percentage viability using Eq. (1).

Water and solvent-based ink formulations were prepared using FNH as pigment for flexography and screen printing, respectively. The first step in the formulation of water-based ink involved the milling of FNH (13.70%), acrylic emulsion 1 (68.47%), carbitol (15.64%) and wetting & dispersing agent (2.19%) in a Llyod's pigment muller to obtain a homogenous dispersion of the ink concentrate or the mill base. Further, the remaining components (Table 1 ) were added to the mill base and mixed thoroughly to achieve the uniformly dispersed water-based ink formulation for flexography. The particle size was measured by placing a small amount of the ink on the Hegman gauge. The solvent-based ink formulation for screen printing involved simple blending of FNH with the screen varnish, which was prepared by mixing the screen medium, retarder, and cyclohexanone solvent. Table 1 shows the various components of the ink formulations and their respective percentage compositions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating and printing of ink on substrate
ce:section.ce:para: The coats and prints (both flexo and screen) using the formulated inks were obtained on UV dull paper procured from Manipal Press Pvt Ltd. The thickness (0.121 mm) and grammage (80 gsm) of the substrate were tested using Mitutoyo thickness gauge and Digital GSM tester, respectively. The K bar 0 number coater was used to drawdown an ink film on the paper substrate. Further, the laboratory flexography printing on paper was achieved using RK Flexiproof 100 at 70 m/min speed with a photopolymer plate, 9.5 BCM anilox roll and a chambered doctor blade inking system. The screen printing was done using a 120-mesh screen (120 openings per inch) and a squeeze on the paper substrate.

The flexo-printed paper samples were cut evenly to 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm dimensions and exposed to UV light (300 W/30 m/min) in a UV curing equipment for varying cycles from 0 to 50 to check the photostability of the ink film. The FL spectra of these samples after every exposure cycle were recorded at ambient conditions. The scratch resistance of the ink film was checked using a Sutherland ink rub tester by setting the tester for various strokes after attaching the unprinted and printed test strips of 8 cm × 5 cm dimensions on the base plate and rubber pad, respectively. A weight of 4 lbs was positioned on the test block rubber pad placed over the printed paper sample. A Xrite I1 Pro instrument was used to measure the colorimetric parameters including L*, a*, b*, and ΔE as well as the density values. The color difference ΔE was calculated using the Eq. (2).

The surface morphology and and topological features of the print proofs were analyzed using Carl Zeiss EVO 18 SEM and INNOVA SPM atomic force microscope (AFM), respectively. Keithley Hall Effect set up (3706 A and room temperature) was used for Hall Effect experiments of the printed samples. The electrochemical measurements were achieved using a PM5, Agilent device analyser B1500A DC probe station with pulsed source of 5 MHz and Agilent 4294A impedance analyzer using silver contacts.

Sebum-rich finger prints were collected from a volunteer by gently rubbing the finger on the face (chin, nose and forehead areas), and then imprinting them on a glass surface. The FNH-g was dusted over the glass surface, and the excess brushed off to uncover the fingerprint [27]. The fingerprint was developed by carefully removing it from the glass surface using a clear adhesive tape, which was further pasted on a paper substrate. The developed substrate was visualized under 365 nm UV light. The photographs were captured using a smartphone (iPhone 12 mini) camera. The 3D interactive plots and the grey value over distance was obtained using image analysis with ImageJ software.

Crystal structure for FNH with monoclinic, space group P21/c, a = 18.9004(6)Å, b = 12.6263(4) Å, c = 7.7258(2)Å, V = 1838.74(10)Å3, Z = 4, T = 300(2)K, μ(Mo Kα) = 0.088 mm−1, Dcalc = 1.367 g/cm3, 26,525 measured reflections (3.88° ≤ 2θ ≤ 56.59°), 4571 unique (R int = 0.0591, Rsigma = 0.0533) was used in all calculations. The final R factor and wR 2 were 0.0545 (I> = 2σ(I)) and 0.1157, respectively for all data. The crystal data and the structure refinement details of FNH are summarized in Table 2 . The thermal ellipsoid diagram with 50% probability is shown in Fig. 1 a. The Schiff base contains a fluorene unit (rings A, B, and C in Fig. 1a), and a naphthalene unit (rings D and E in Fig. 1a), and both units are connected through CH = N-NH-CO linkage. The –OH moiety is attached to the 3rd position of the naphthalene unit. Both the naphthalene and fluorene units adopt planar conformations. The plane of naphthalene deviates from that of fluorene with an angle of 18.75° (Fig. 1b). The small deviation between the planes of naphthalene and fluorene units might be due to the presence of a strong N2-H1AA…O2 S(6) intramolecular H-bond. C17-H17…O1 is another S(6) weak intra-molecular H-bond observed in FNH. O2-H1…O1 is a strong H-bond between neighboring molecules that significantly stabilizes FNH (Table 2 and Fig. 1c). The O1 atom engages in yet another intermolecular H-bond interaction; C20-H20…O1 (Table 2 and Fig. 1c). The molecular packing of FNH is shown in Fig. 1d (view down along the C axis). It is evident from the molecular packing and H-bond networking along b direction in Fig. 1e that the H-bonds are dominating along b direction, whereas van der waals interactions are leading in the other two directions. The naphthalene units of the adjacent FNH molecules are connected through intermolecular H-bonds (strong O2-H1…O1, weak C20-H20…O1). The fluorene moieties are parallelly interconnected by the weaker C9-H9A…π H-bonding (Table 3 ). In addition, two other CH…π interactions also contribute to stabilize the packing of FNH.

To obtain deeper insights into the intermolecular interactions of FNH, HS and 2D fingerprint plots were generated (Crystal Explorer - Version 17.5). The HS contains two brighter spots corresponding to O2H1…O1 H-bonds (Fig. 2 a). There are no other significant brighter spots, which indicates that the O2H1…O1 H-bond with neighboring molecules plays a major role in the stability of FNH compared to other interactions. The 2D fingerprint plots from HS surfaces are obtained by plotting the fraction of surface points as a function of de vs di, where de and di denote the distances from the point on the HS to the adjacent atoms outside and inside the surface, correspondingly. The complete fingerprint plot (Fig. 2b) along with the plots demonstrating the various contacts of FNH: All-H (71.1%; Fig. 2c), All-N (2.4% Fig. 2d), All-O (5.3%; Fig. 2e), All-C (21.2%; Fig. 2f), H-All (64.0%; Fig. 2g), HC (33%; Fig. 2h), HH (45.3%; Fig. 2i), HN (3.5%; Fig. 2j), HO (7.7%; Fig. 2k), and NN (0.0%; Fig. 2l) are shown. The maximum contribution of contacts established by FNH according to divided fingerprints are H…H (45.3%), H…C or C…H (33.0%), and H…O or O…H (7.7%). The sharp spikes developed for H…All and O…All in the fingerprint plots indicate the formation of dimers (bifurcated H-bonding network). The 33.0% contribution of CH and HC interactions validate the importance of CH…O and CH…π interactions in the stability of FNH molecules.

The energy framework calculations enable to comprehend detailed evidences on crystal packing and envisage the interaction network topologies attributed to the conformational changes of FNH. B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) electron-density functions were employed to calculate the electrostatic (Eele), dispersion (Edis), polarization (Epol), and exchange-repulsion interaction energies between FNH molecules. The two most significant pairwise energy values are −64.9, −61.3, and −60.4 kJ/mol. The top two electrostatic and dispersion energies are −77.2 & −17.9 kJ/mol, and −94.4 & −61.4 kJ/mol (Table S2), respectively. The major energy interactions are between the NH of the molecules. Overall, the Eele interactions are significant (Fig. 2m), and the Edis contributions are dominant (Fig. 2n). The FNH crystal structure stabilizes with total energy (Etot) and engages in a network of interactions in the ab plane (Fig. 2o).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intrinsic optical features of FNH pigment
ce:section.ce:para: As FNH was designed and synthesized to be used as a functional fluorescent pigment in ink formulation, the characteristic emission behavior was explored in detail.

FNH was soluble in THF, DMF and DMSO, and the absorption spectra (1 × 10−5 M) of respective solutions recorded is shown in Fig. 3 a. Two absorbance peaks at around 270 and 340 nm are ascribed to π → π* and n → π* transitions, respectively [28–30]. The emission spectra of FNH in these solvents (1 × 10−3 M) as presented in Fig. 3b were recorded at λex = 400 nm. Two emission peaks were observed at 494 and 535 nm in DMF, corresponding to yellowish-green FL as perceived in the CIE diagram (Fig. 3c). However, DMSO and THF solutions displayed orange FL (Fig. 3b and 3c). It is reported that a flexible molecular structure favours non-radiative decay from the excited state, whereas radiative relaxation is enhanced if the structure is rigid [31,32]. However, in the aggregated state wherein the molecules are closer, the free rotations are blocked and the energy decay through non-radiative pathways are restricted to result in strong FL emission [31,33]. Therefore, the emission behaviors of FNH in THF-water mixtures were checked by altering the water (poor solvent) content (f w = 0–99%) to examine the aggregation process and in turn the FL features of the luminophore. The rigid structure due to intramolecular H-bonding and the restricted intramolecular motions of FNH can promote AIE via radiative relaxation channels through formation of maximum number of aggregates. Fig. 3d displays the photographs of FNH in THF-water mixtures in daylight and under 365 nm UV light, whereas Fig. 3e shows the respective emission plots at λex = 370 nm. The emission of FNH in pure THF (λem = 618 nm) displays a blue shift to 494 nm with increasing water fractions, which perhaps could be due to the formation of H-aggregates. Though H-aggregates usually results in a parallel molecular arrangement that can quench the FL, imperfect stacking and inclined molecular geometry in H-aggregates in some cases can allow FL [34,35]. The intensity of the emission at 494 nm reached a maximum when f w = 90%, which is due to the formation of aggregates and not the hydrolysis of the imine. It is well-established that the H-bonds play a critical role in preventing the hydrolysis of imines in water [36]. The resistance of FNH to hydrolysis could be attributed to the strong intramolecular H-bond (N2-H1AA…O2) illustrated in crystal studies.The shifts in emission wavelengths with increasing water fractions are evident from the chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3f). The spectral variations (λex = 370 nm) observed upon photoexcitation demonstrated the aggregation of FNH in aqueous mixtures having high water content indicating typical AIE characteristics, which was further confirmed by SEM imaging of f w = 0% and 90% samples. The microimages of f w = 0% sample displayed scattered particles (Fig. 3g) whereas that of f w = 90% exhibited fibre like aggregates (Fig. 3h). Further, the average particle sizes of the samples (f w = 0 and 90%) checked using DLS measurements displayed 20.2 and 1981 nm, respectively, which confirms the aggregate formation of FNH (Fig. S5).

The density functional theory (DFT) studies of FNH were performed to generate the optimized molecular geometry and analyze the electronic distribution in molecular orbital energy levels (Fig. 4 a). The electron density is localized only on the fluorene and spacer moiety in the HOMO, with an energy value of −5.27 eV. However, the charge distribution is seen on the entire molecule in LUMO, but more shifted to the naphthalene unit and the spacer, with an energy value of −1.44 eV. As the DFT studies suggest the possibility of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) phenomenon in FNH, the emission features of the molecule were recorded in polar THF - nonpolar hexane (1 × 10−4 M) mixtures. A huge hypsochromic shift of ∼200 nm with intense emission was observed upon increasing the hexane content, which indicated the ICT process in the molecule (Fig. 4b) [37].

The pristine FNH powder did not show any visible FL under 365 nm UV light as opposed to the strong emission expected from this AIE luminophore. Intriguingly, upon grinding using a mortar and pestle, a visible color change of FNH from off-white to pale yellow in daylight and an orangish-yellow FL under UV light, which disappeared on heating was observed. To confirm these observations, the solid-state FL of pristine FNH, and ground FNH before (FNH-g) and after heating (FNH-h) were recorded for λex = 410 nm as presented in (Fig. 4c). Two emission peaks were observed for FNH at 494 and 535 nm, whereas the higher wavelength emission displayed a red shift to 590 nm upon grinding in FNH-g, along with a minor emission peak at 494 nm. Further heating of FNH-g indicated a reversal of FL emission (FNH-h) similar to FNH (Fig. 4c). The chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4d) for FNH, FNH-g and FNH-h displays cyanish-green, orange and yellowish-green FL with chromaticity coordinates (x, y) of (0.254, 0.397), (0.449, 0.436) and (0.317, 0.443) respectively, showing the tendency of FNH-g to revert to FNH upon heating. Further, the excitation spectra at 440 and 480 nm for FNH and FNH-g were monitored at different emissions (Fig. 4e) to perceive a remarkable observation. As depicted in Fig. 4f, Both FNH and FNH-g displayed two emission peaks at 494 nm (major peak) and 535 nm (minor peak) for the 480 nm excitation, but the intensities of the FNH-g emission peaks were lower than that of FNH. However, when excited at 440 nm, both the emission peaks at 494 and 535 nm were of equal peak height, but the intensities of the FNH-g was lower than FNH. The corresponding chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4g) vividly represents the varying green emissions from both FNH and FNH-g at 440 and 480 nm excitation.

To comprehend the mechanism of mechanical force-induced FL behavior, initially FTIR spectra of FNH and FNH-g as shown in Fig. 5 a were analyzed. Both the spectra displayed similar vibrational peaks except for the disappearance of the OH peak centered at 3500 cm−1 in FNH-g. This confirmed the structural integrity of FNH even after subjecting to mechanical force, but indicated the destruction of H-bonds upon grinding [38]. Further, PXRD and DSC measurements were performed to inspect the plausible phase transformations. PXRD data of equal masses of FNH and FNH-g were examined for better comparison. The sharp reflection peaks (Fig. 5b) indicate the crystalline nature of the as-synthesized FNH powder. After grinding, all the diffraction peaks are found to broaden with their reflection intensity significantly reduced, suggesting a significant disruption of the crystal lattice [20,39]. The pristine FNH displayed a sharp endothermic peak (heat capacity: 156.04 mJ; enthalpy: 59.40 J/g) at 284.57 ℃, corresponding to its transition to isotropic liquid (Fig. 5c). Upon grinding FNH, a cold-crystallization peak was observed at 130 ℃ (heat capacity: 25.23 mJ; enthalpy: 70.68 J/g). This indicates the formation of a metastable state in FNH-g, which can revert to more stable crystalline form by an annealing process [10,19,20]. The optical microscope images depicting the crystalline FNH and less crystalline FNH-g are presented in Fig. 5d. The visual morphological changes after grinding were further confirmed by collecting the SEM images (Fig. S6) of the pristine and the ground samples.

The strong intramolecular NH…O and intermolecular OH…O H-bonds account for the nearly planar sheet like structure of FNH as observed from the SCXRD data. Upon grinding, these H-bonds can get disturbed as detected in the FTIR analysis. The intramolecular NH…O bond length may change, and there are possibilities that the molecule can twist or bend. Thus the H-bonds play a major role in the new conformation adopted by FNH-g, which leads to different emission behavior [40,41]. In order to support the key role played by the aromatic OH group involved in H-bonds, concentration-dependent 1H NMR spectra were recorded for 10 and 30 mM concentrations of FNH in DMSO‑d6 (Fig. 5e) [20]. Upon increasing the concentration, the OH proton signal shifted from 12.070 to 12.034 ppm (0.036 ppm), which confirms the intermolecular H-bonding existing between the aromatic OH and carbonyl O from two adjacent molecules. On the contrary, a slight variation in the chemical shift of the imine proton (NH) from 11.369 to 11.373 (0.004 ppm) indicates its participation only in intramolecular H-bonding with the aromatic OH. An analogous conclusion may also be extended for the mechanofluorochromism exhibited by FNH in its solid-state. Moreover, the application of mechanical force may enable a transition from stable crystalline FNH assemblies to a metastable semi-crystalline FNH-g state as observed in the DSC curves. This transformation can alter the molecular packing, thereby resulting in different FL emissions from FNH and FNH-g. Thus, the variations in H-bonding and packing arrangement could have played a critical role in the origin of different emission behaviors from FNH and FNH-g.

Surprisingly, yet another unique phenomenon: excitation wavelength dependent emission trails, which are dissimilar for FNH and FNH-g were observed. FNH and FNH-g demonstrated a different emission path (in the FL color) when excited at different wavelengths ranging from 250 to 480 nm as portrayed in Fig. 6 a and 6b. The FL color transformed from purple to cyan and finally to green in the case of FNH, whereas an entirely different emission trail from purplish cyan to orange, yellow, greenish-yellow, and finally to green was observed for FNH-g upon excitation from low to high wavelength ranges.

As the OH and NH groups as well as the imine N in FNH are susceptible to deprotonation and protonation, the pH response of FNH was explored in acidic and basic environment using H2SO4/NaOH. The respective photographs in daylight and under UV light (365 nm) are shown in Fig. S7. As the pH value is increased from 2 to 10 the colorless FNH solution changed to yellow, and the FL emission showed a red shift as depicted in Fig. 6c. The emission color moved from blue to cyan to green as displayed in the CIE plots (Fig. 6d). Moreover, upon further decreasing the pH, the solution reverts to colorless. This process was reversible, which suggests good material stability to varying pH. The pH sensitive FL exhibited by FNH can be ascribed to the modulating ICT through protonation/deprotonation process.

Decomposition of pigments can reduce its commercial usefulness. As FNH is synthesized for use as a colorant material in security inks, study of its temperature induced degradation is crucial. TGA analysis is a well-established technique to comprehend the phase transitions of a fluorophore and provides insights into its thermal stability. The TGA plots (Fig. 6e) portraying the weight percentage against temperature demonstrates the thermal stability of FNH up to 300 °C. The TGA curves indicated two weight loss steps: initial 40% weight loss around 300 ℃ could be due to the initial disintegration and later ∼20 % mass loss at 435 ℃ could be attributed to the gradual degradation of FNH framework [42,43].

The schiff base FNH did not demonstrate any significant toxicity up to 30 µm in MTT assay as presented in Fig. 6f. This proves its efficacy to be used as a biocompatible pigment in the ink formulations.

FNH, which displayed unique emission behavior upon photoexcitation was used as a pigment to prepare i) water-based ink for flexo (Fig. S8a) and ii) solvent-based ink for screen printing (Fig. S8b). The water-based ink prepared for flexography had a particle size of ∼5 μm and pH around 7–8. As per the ISO 12647-6 standards (L*: ≥90, a*: ≤3 and b*: ≤5), UV dull paper (having no optical brighteners) with L*, a*, b* and density values of 90.77, 0.16, 2.8 and 0.02, respectively were used as printing substrates. A swatch of ink was coated across the paper by drawdown technique for initial visual analysis and to check the performance of the ink film including i) the compatibility between various constituents of the ink, ii) the quality of color matching and mixing, and iii) the drying properties of the ink on the substrate surface before laboratory printing. The wet film deposit after coating with flexo ink was 4 µm.

Furthermore, a 105 mm × 297 mm solid patch was flexo printed on the paper, which dried at room temperature (within 1–2 min after printing) to obtain a 5 µm thick ink film. The CIELAB color spaces that are proposed to estimate human vision are used to describe the colors observed from the printed/coated paper samples. Thechromaticity parameter L*closely matches human perception of color lightness, values ranging from 0 to 100 (black and white, respectively). The a* and b* values vary from −120 to 120: negative a* implies green and positive indicates red, whereas negative b* suggests blue and positive represents yellow. The ΔE represents the color difference; if the value range is 0–2 then it is invisible, can be recognized by experts and any individual if the values are 2–3 and >3, respectively [44–46]. Table 4 lists the colorimetric values for the coated and printed substrates. The L* value (90.77) for the unprinted/uncoated substrate, which is close to 100 suggests that the color perceived is white. Though the L* values of coated papers are close to blank, the increasing positive b* values which is greater than the negative a* values suggest that the dominating color of the ink film obtained through coating is yellow (Fig. 7 a) [47–49]. This observation is further confirmed by the slight rise in the ΔE values for the samples with increasing number of coatings. However the coats/prints were invisible in daylight as evidenced by the ΔE values. The graphical representation of the color spaces comparing the coated (C1) and printed (P1) samples is shown in Fig. 7b.

The features exhibited by the prints are very crucial for commercial application. The light fastness of the pigment is one of the most important visual properties majorly sought after in inks. Therefore, the resistance to fading of color and FL under the influence of light source was examined by recording the CIELAB color values after subjecting the prints to different cycles of UV exposure. The printed samples displayed increasing positive b* values, which indicate yellowing upon increasing UV exposure (Fig. 7c). Rub or scuff resistance which defines the ability of the print to endure rubbing, smudging, marking or scuffing during various handling processes, including packaging, distribution and usage is yet another crucial parameter that decides the print quality. The resistance to abrasion of the ink film on the paper surface was examined by checking the L*, a*, b * values of the prints after subjecting to 25, 50, 75 and 100 strokes. Table 4 shows the colorimetric values for the rub tested papers, and the respective pictorial representation is depicted in Fig. 7d. The insignificant changes in the L*, a*, b* and ΔE values after the rub test compared to unrubbed sample vividly proves the good rub resistance of the ink film on the substrate.

The surface morphology of blank and printed paper samples were studied by SEM and AFM analysis. The microimages of the surface view of the printed paper samples showed homogenous areas [49]. Comparison of the surface images (Fig. 7e and 7f) of blank and printed papers presented a relatively smooth ink film which fills the porous and fibrous texture of the cellulosic paper. The 3D surface topography of printed sample was further analyzed by AFM in comparison with the unprinted paper (Fig. 7g and 7 h). The average surface roughness (Ra), root mean square roughness (Rq) and maximum roughness depth (Rmax) values reduced from 352, 439 and 3115 nm in blank to 157, 203 and 1348 nm after printing.
As FNH demonstrated several unique optical features, the ink film obtained on UV dull paper was explored for security features that could be used in real life anticounterfeiting.

Level 1 non-destructive approach for authentication of print: The inked area appears invisible in daylight with weak emission upon illumination under 365 nm UV light source (Fig. S9a and S9b), which the forger observes. However, the user can authenticate the originality of the document by rubbing the print with hard objects, especially using a metal coin or glass rod to perceive a human eye-detectable intensification in the orange FL under UV light source as presented in Fig. 8 a and Video. Further, the emission spectra (λex = 370 nm) of the flexo prints were analyzed before and after rubbing to confirm the visually perceived FL features (Fig. 8). The rubbed samples displayed a bathochromically shifted emission, as observed in the respective chromaticity diagram. Morever, to meet the requirements for real-life multiple-time utility, the fluorophore should easily revert to its original state from the perturbed form. As desired, the orange FL reverts to the original upon heating, which enables the reuse of the printed security document/product after authentication (Fig. 8c). Furthermore, the screen prints with ‘MAHE’ printed on UV dull paper also demonstrated the rub induced FL phenomenon as shown in Figs. S10a and S10b. The rub-induced FL emission and heat-induced recovery of the original emission are evidenced using the emission spectra in Fig. S11. As the FNH ink film demonstrated stable secondary enhancement in FL upon rubbing in combination with good transparency in the visible region, these formulations can function as invisible security inks to check the originality of documents/passports/branded products etc. The rub induced FL acts as a first level authenticating feature, which is only known to the user but hidden for the forger. Moreover, the reversible nature of FL upon simple annealing can be used for multiple authentications without tampering with the printed sample. The text printed on the paper shows consistent and reproducible results under ambient conditions. In addition, both the formulations can be used as invisible inks to store information, encrypt secret communication and have a substantial potential in anticounterfeiting [50].

Level 2 non-destructive approach for authentication of print: As FNH and FNH-g demonstrated dissimilar trails upon shining with different wavelengths, the emission of the screen prints before and after rubbing were recorded and the respective color coordinates are presented in the CIE plots (Fig. 8d and 8e). The screen print (SP) displayed a trail from blue to cyan to green emission color when excited at 250 to 470 nm (Fig. 8d), whereas the rubbed screen print showed a different emission path (from purple to orange, yellow, yellowish-green and then green) for the same excitation (Fig. 8e), which is a unique and fascinating feature. The print thus emits an orangish yellow FL from the rubbed region and cyan FL from the unrubbed region of the print when shined with 270–400 nm UV light source, whereas the print emits cyanish-green FL from both the unrubbed and rubbed regions when illuminated with a visible light source ranging from 420 to 470 nm. This adds to its higher-level covert security feature for product/document authentication.

Level 3 destructive approach for authentication of print: The solvent and pH induced color/FL changes under visible and UV light of FNH that could be viewed using naked eyes was further exploited as yet another approach to confirm the originality of a document [51]. Initially, the influence of THF, DMF and DMSO solvents on the print was analyzed by exposing them to 1–2 drops of these solvents. No significant changes were observed on the prints treated with DMF and DMSO under daylight, as well as under 365 nm UV illumination. However, a bright red FL under 365 nm illumination with no surface discoloration in daylight was perceived from the printed region exposed to THF (Fig. S12). Moreover, a unique and distinct penetrating red FL emission was perceived from the rear side of the THF exposed region of the printed paper (Fig. S12). This observation is supported by the emission spectra: the printed side treated with THF (P1 + THF; Fig. 8f) and rear side (P1 + THF rear; Fig. 8g), which displayed a new emission peak at 580 nm. Similarly, the printed area treated with NaOH showed a yellow emission (Fig. 8h), while there was no penetrating FL observed from the rear side (Fig. 8i) of the base exposed region, which is supported by the emission spectra.

As FNH demonstrated low cytotoxicity, the water-based ink formulation could be used in thumbprint ink pads and stamp-pads to efficiently detect fraud and allow safety of documents. As the prints displayed smooth surfaces, the electrical parameters including intrinsic charge transport features and mobility were elucidated for its prospective use in electronic anticounterfeiting. The flexo-printed paper sample (thickness of paper 0.114 mm and ink film 0.002 mm) displayed n-type semiconducting feature with a Hall coefficient of −362887.48 cm3/C, carrier density of 1.72 × 1013 cm−3, mobility of 7.31 cm2/Vs, electrical conductivity of 2.02 × 10−5 cm−1/Vs, bulk conductivity of 8.10 × 10−5 Sm−1 and resistivity of 1.23 × 104 Ωm. The values suggest that the formulated water-based flexo ink can be used to easily fabricate thin-film organic electrochemical transistor using printing technology. This type of electronic devices fabricated directly on the surface of confidential documents/currency notes to implement active security features can be further explored as not only the substrate is eco-friendly and flexible, but also the device performance is less sensitive to substrate roughness and needs low operating voltages.

Fingerprint analysis or marking technologies is a reliable and important method for individual identity in forensic science [52–54]. Hence, the applicability of FNH as a marker for developing and visualizing LFPs was investigated. LFPs vary with individuals and contain various intrinsic constituents, both organic compounds including amino acids, peptides and fatty acids and inorganic ions such as chloride and phosphate [55,56] as well as other extrinsic contaminants including dirt, cosmetics, grease, food contaminants etc. The ridge patterns of the finger leave a LFP whenever touched on a solid surface. The LFPs were developed as mentioned in the experimental section and could be visualized as presented in Fig. 9 a. The distribution of FNH-g on the ridges rather than the furrow areas is vividly evident from the noticeably larger grey scale value in the ridges and significantly lower in furrow regions (Fig. 9b). Moreover, the extracted 3D interactive profile of LFP as depicted in Fig. 9c clearly shows the uniform distribution of FNH-g. The ridges and valleys are the most evident structural elements of FPs in forensic investigations, and these traits are classified as three levels [57,58]: i) basic level 1 characteristics that are not distinctive are insufficient for personal identification, ii) level 2 features include the macroscopic elements and iii) level 3 incorporates the microscopic elements. The level 1 features including core and delta are visible in the photographs of LFPs developed using FNH-g when viewed under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 9d). Bifurcation, cross-over and ridge endings are the level 2 details that are clearly distinguishable as presented in Fig. 9d, which facilitates a legal and reliable evidence for individualization. The level 3 features are not very clearly highlighted. However, as the currently used luminophoric LFP developers are often hazardous compounds [53], biocompatible and easy to synthesize FNH-g can be explored further as a safer material for developing LFPs.

This comprehensive study demonstrates the synthesis and characterization of a force responsive Schiff base FNH that incorporates fluorene and hydroxy naphthalene into its molecular design to serve as a single functional pigment in invisible inks that display unique multilevel security features for safety and authentication of documents/passports/branded products. The molecule demonstrated several striking features such as AIE, ICT, mechanofluorochromism, solvent/pH sensitivity as well as good thermal and color stabilities. Low-cost and durable ink formulations with low FNH loading was successfully printed onto UV dull paper by flexography and screen-printing techniques to obtain prints with good adhesion, color stability, and abrasion resistance. The covert security features were non-destructive allowing reuse of documents, as well as destructive in nature for thorough verification of document/product originality and prevents easy duplication by forger. The prints which are invisible in daylight exhibits rub induced FL; i) orangish yellow FL from the rubbed region and cyan FL from the unrubbed region of the print when shined with 270–400 nm light source, ii) cyanish-green FL from both the unrubbed and rubbed areas when illuminated with a visible light source ranging from 420 to 470 nm and iii) THF or NaOH/KOH sensitive/penetrating FL from the prints. The reversible nature of the FL emission of the rubbed invisible print activated by heating offers a feasible semi-covert security element for strategically constructing fluorescent texts in information encryption & decryption, coding, fingerprint technology and anticounterfeit labels signifying its promising applicability in data security and advanced anticounterfeiting. Moreover, the electrical parameter values obtained for the prints highlighted the potential prospects of the prepared inks in flexible printed devices for electronic anticounterfeiting. The LFP images developed and visualized by powder dusting method using FNH-g revealed level 1–2 ridge details for personal identity in forensic application, which can be further explored.
Indian patent (Application No. 202341036330) has been filed for part of this study.
